action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before, and you
have NO examples to start from. Your task is to work out how it works entirely on your
own, and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: dog, tree, hill, boat, stone, farmer, spear, road, king, bird, hunter, ship, lake, river
Adjectives: dark, old, big, blue, tall, small, red, cold
Verbs (write them in this base form): hold, carry, feed, watch, hear, chase, find, see
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a dog hold a tree
    six dogs hold a tree
    a dark dog hold a tree

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. You are starting from nothing, so spend your early probes establishing the
   basic words and word order, then test how number, case, definiteness, agreement and
   any sound changes work. Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
